title: Green alga Ulva lactuca polysaccharide inhibits hepatocellular carcinoma through miRNA-mediated regulation and metabolite modulation.
author_string: Wu, Yinfeng, Jia, Nan, Chen, Yaobin, Guo, Yuxin, Qiu, Yinghui et al.
abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) lacks sufficient treatment options, highlighting the urgent need for new and effective therapeutic agents. This study systematically explored the multidimensional molecular mechanisms underlying the antitumor activity of Ulva lactuca polysaccharide (ULP), a natural marine product. miRNA sequencing revealed that ULP significantly suppresses the expression of the proto-oncogene microRNA miR-212-5p in HCC tissues. Transcriptomic analysis, combined with ULP as a direct target of miR-212-5p, demonstrated that miR-212-5p is upregulated by its precursor, and when the precursor is inhibited, miR-212-5p expression increases. This establishes the primary regulatory axis through which ULP exerts its effects. Additionally, metabolomics analysis showed that ULP reshapes the intestinal metabolic profile in HCC model mice, with its effects closely linked to the regulation of the purine metabolic pathway. Integration of transcriptomic and metabolomic data led to the construction of a gene-metabolite interaction network, identifying key downstream effector genes. This study reveals that ULP operates through a complex network, involving the miR-212-5p/Man2a1 axis and metabolic remodeling, to inhibit HCC. These findings provide a robust theoretical foundation for ULP as a potential therapeutic agent for HCC and highlight multiple targets for future research.
